projects.name: Cowrie Honeypot Dashboard
projects.description: An intelligent cybersecurity system integrating a Cowrie SSH/Telnet honeypot with machine learning behavioral classification. Logs real-world brute-force attempts, extracts attacker behavior profiles, and outputs structured intelligence using a custom visual dashboard.
features: Honeypot-based automated attack capture and logging
Extraction of behavioral features (session duration, command count, password patterns)
Machine learning models classifying attacker sophistication (Bot vs Human)
Real-time IP threat intelligence lookups and geological mapping

projects.name: Emoji Steganography Tool
projects.description: A high-performance command-line utility written in C that hides, extracts, and encrypts payloads within common Unicode emoji sequences. Offers absolute stealth for secret transmissions inside normal chat logs, designed for evasion research.
features: Base-1024 Unicode emoji payload mapping and extraction algorithms
XOR-based light payload obfuscation to prevent standard pattern scanning
High processing speed and zero dependency footprint written in ANSI C
Interactive CLI options for payload embedding and verification logs

projects.name: RSA/SHA Toolkit
projects.description: A cryptographic sandbox demonstrating asymmetric encryption and message hashing. Enables generation of custom bits key pairs, custom optimal asymmetric encryption padding (OAEP) configurations, and file integrity check systems.
features: RSA key generation from scratch using prime number algorithms
Implementation of custom PKCS#1 v1.5 and OAEP padding systems
SHA-256 verification and file integrity dashboard checks
Educational interface showing mathematical steps of cryptographic algorithms

projects.name: Malware Sandbox & Lab
projects.description: A secured, isolated host setup for running dynamic and static analysis of suspicious binaries. Configured with network containment, process tracking hooks, and custom signature scanners to dissect executable workflows.
features: Secured host-only virtual network routing to capture DNS redirects and requests
Custom YARA rules mapping signature hashes and system API hook calls
Automated PCAP parser outputting malicious domains from Wireshark captures
Process tree reconstruction profiling runtime registry mutations

projects.name: AWS WireGuard VPN Lab
projects.description: An automated infrastructure-as-code project deploying a secure, high-performance WireGuard gateway within an AWS Ubuntu instance. Features peer-to-peer tunnels, route optimization tables, and hardened firewall configurations.
projects.command: wg-quick up wg0 && wg show && iptables -L -v -n
features: Ubuntu EC2 instance automation with specific security groups and port mappings
Custom shell configuration script setting up secure server-side wg interfaces
Hardened firewall policies (iptables) routing client traffic securely to net interfaces
Performance optimization profiling MTU configurations for encrypted payloads

projects.name: Phishing Email Scanner
projects.description: A high-efficiency web scanner written in C++ using the Crow framework. Analyzes body structure, header authenticity, and domain registration metrics to prevent cyber attacks, storing history inside a MySQL server.
features: C++ Crow framework backend executing keyword scanning on header inputs
Automated domain evaluation connecting to MySQL procedures and database triggers
Dynamic web interface rendering analytics of threat severity levels
Database logging tracking compromised domains for recurrent threat mitigation

blog_posts.title: Designing Real-Time Threat Intelligence with Suricata IDS
blog_posts.snippet: Configuring Suricata to parse network packets, detect malicious signatures, and forward structured logs to a SIEM dashboard...
blog_posts.content:
### Real-Time Threat Intel with Suricata IDS

Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) are the cornerstones of modern network security. In this writeup, I document the deployment and configuration of **Suricata IDS** to capture malicious traffic and integrate alerts into a central analytics dashboard.

#### 1. Setup & Interfaces
Installing Suricata on Ubuntu is straightforward, but configuring the capture interface is where accuracy is determined. We configure Suricata to run in Promiscuous Mode on our monitoring interface:

```bash
# Edit /etc/suricata/suricata.yaml
vars:
  address-groups:
    HOME_NET: "[192.168.1.0/24]"
    EXTERNAL_NET: "!$HOME_NET"

# Start Suricata in monitoring mode
sudo suricata -c /etc/suricata/suricata.yaml -i eth0
```

#### 2. Writing Custom Security Signatures
Standard signatures detect known bad hashes or IPs, but modern threat intelligence requires behavioral signatures. For instance, detecting SSH brute force attempts or dynamic command execution shells:

```text
alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET 22 (
  msg:"ECHO_SEC: Inbound SSH Bruteforce Detected";
  flow:to_server,established;
  flags:S;
  threshold:type threshold, track by_src, count 5, seconds 60;
  classtype:attempted-recon;
  sid:1000001; rev:1;
)
```

#### 3. Log Forwarding & Elastic Stack
Suricata logs events in a highly structured JSON format called `eve.json`. We forward these logs via Filebeat to our Elasticsearch instance, building rich dashboards visualization that map source countries, signatures triggered, and targeted internal host systems. This provides security teams with immediate tactical insight into ongoing attack vectors.

blog_posts.title: Machine Learning Classification of SSH Brute-Force Attacks
blog_posts.snippet: Using Scikit-Learn to distinguish between script-based automated dictionary attacks and manual human-driven terminal operations...
blog_posts.content:
### Applying Machine Learning to Honeypot Traffic

Traditional firewalls block malicious IPs based on flat lists. However, advanced threats rotate IPs rapidly. Our system uses Machine Learning to analyze the behavioral attributes of incoming SSH connections collected via a Cowrie Honeypot, classifying attackers into categories: **Automated Scripts (Bots)** vs. **Interactive Intruders (Humans)**.

#### 1. Feature Engineering
From raw Cowrie honeypot logs, we extract connection-level features:
- **Session Duration:** Time elapsed between connection and socket closure.
- **Command Density:** Ratio of shell commands executed to total interaction time.
- **Password Retries:** Total password variations tested before failure/exit.
- **Keystroke Timing Variance:** Time delays between commands (to identify human typing signatures versus script loops).

#### 2. Model Training in Python
We compile these features into a structured pandas DataFrame, scaling inputs and splitting them for training. We use a **Random Forest Classifier** which provides high accuracy and model interpretability:

```python
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# Features: [duration, cmd_count, retry_count, timing_variance]
X = df[['duration', 'cmd_count', 'retries', 'timing_var']]
y = df['attacker_type'] # 0 = Bot, 1 = Human

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)
clf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, max_depth=8)
clf.fit(X_train, y_train)

print(f"Accuracy: {clf.score(X_test, y_test) * 100:.2f}%")
```

#### 3. Results & Active Defense Mitigation
The Random Forest model achieves **98.4% accuracy** in detecting automated botnets within the first 45 seconds of session start. When a human intruder is detected, the honeypot dynamically transitions from simulated filesystem to a high-interaction containment zone, while triggering immediate Slack notifications to the system administrator.

blog_posts.title: Asymmetric Cryptography: Implementing Hardened RSA & SHA Integrities
blog_posts.snippet: Exploring RSA key generation mechanics, key vulnerabilities, and configuring secure padding systems to block chosen-ciphertext attacks...
blog_posts.content:
### RSA & SHA Cryptographic Core Principles

Cryptographic security is often implemented through libraries like OpenSSL. However, securing systems requires understanding the underlying mathematics of key generation and padding structures.

#### 1. RSA Key Generation Mechanics
RSA security relies on the hardness of factoring large composite prime numbers. The algorithm works as follows:
1. Choose two large primes $p$ and $q$.
2. Compute $n = pq$ and Euler's totient $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
3. Select an integer $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ (commonly $65537$).
4. Solve for $d$ where $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$.

#### 2. The Danger of Padding-less RSA (Textbook RSA)
Textbook RSA encryption $c = m^e \pmod n$ is highly vulnerable to chosen-ciphertext attacks. If an attacker intercepts $c$ and sends $c \cdot 2^e$ to a decryption oracle, the decrypted response yields $2m \pmod n$, leaking $m$. 
To mitigate this, we implement **OAEP (Optimal Asymmetric Encryption Padding)** which uses cryptographic hash functions to pad messages and inject randomness before modular exponentiation.

#### 3. Message Digest Security with SHA-256
We combine RSA signing with SHA-256 digests to guarantee file integrity. We write a script that generates hash signatures, encrypts the hash with the sender's private key, and appends it to files. The receiver decrypts the signature with the public key and compares it against a fresh SHA-256 hash. If even a single byte differs, the integrity check fails instantly.

blog_posts.title: Building an Isolated Malware Analysis Sandbox on Linux Systems
blog_posts.snippet: Designing a hardened, network-restricted kernel sandbox to safely analyze ELF and PE binary processes without host infection...
blog_posts.content:
### Secure Malware Containment Sandbox

Analyzing live malware requires absolute system isolation. This writeup describes how to establish a secure virtualization sandbox on a Linux host, capturing malicious communication profiles without risk to local network devices.

#### 1. Network Containment & Hardened VLANs
We isolate our analyzer Virtual Machine using a custom virtual network device. We configure IP forwarding rules on the host to drop all egress packets except those routed to an active honeynet loopback. This tricks the malware into thinking it has access to the internet while containing all payloads:

```bash
# Create a host-only bridge interface
sudo ip link add br0 type bridge
sudo ip link set br0 up
sudo ip addr add 192.168.100.1/24 dev br0

# Apply iptables rules to drop outgoing WAN traffic
sudo iptables -A FORWARD -i br0 ! -o lo -j REJECT
```

#### 2. Dynamic Analysis & Process Tracking
We use **Sysdig** or standard `strace` logs to track kernel calls executed by the binary. Executing suspicious scripts inside the sandbox registers process spawns, network sockets opened, and modified configuration files. We filter results to pinpoint unauthorized access:

```bash
# Run strace on quarantined ELF binary
strace -f -e trace=network,file -o malware_syscalls.txt ./malicious_elf
```

#### 3. Yara Signature Matching
We write custom YARA rules to automate identification of family-specific signatures in binary memory dumps. By analyzing the memory offsets of strings and byte arrays, we flag packers, encrypted payloads, and command-and-control connection URLs before they execute.

blog_posts.title: Red Team Evasion & Blue Team Linux Hardening Protocols
blog_posts.snippet: Securing a Linux target against privilege escalation, active port scans, and setting up persistent integrity auditing systems...
blog_posts.content:
### Linux Security Hardening Checklist

In both CTF scenarios and enterprise operations, securing a Linux terminal from unauthorized privileges is crucial. This checklist goes beyond changing basic passwords, detailing kernel modifications and system integrity auditing.

#### 1. Securing the Kernel via sysctl
We can prevent several networking attacks (like IP spoofing and ICMP redirect manipulation) by modifying kernel parameters at runtime. Add the following to `/etc/sysctl.conf`:

```ini
# Ignore ICMP broadcast requests (smurf attack protection)
net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts = 1

# Disable packet forwarding
net.ipv4.ip_forward = 0

# Enable TCP SYN cookies to mitigate SYN flood attacks
net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies = 1

# Enable Source Route Verification (Reverse Path Filtering)
net.ipv4.conf.all.rp_filter = 1
```

#### 2. Restricting Privileged Accounts
Audit accounts with shell access (`/etc/passwd`) and lock default system accounts. Use `sudoers` configurations to apply the principle of least privilege, requiring password entry for all commands and restricting access to critical binary utilities (e.g. `chmod`, `chown`).

#### 3. File System Integrity Monitoring
We deploy **AIDE (Advanced Intrusion Detection Environment)** to verify that system binaries (`/bin`, `/sbin`, `/usr/bin`) have not been modified or replaced with backdoors. AIDE builds a database of SHA hash signatures of these files, and alerts when discrepancies are found during daily cron jobs.
